Just now

Photo by Icarius.jpeg on UnsplashOver two thousand years ago, the Stoic philosopher Seneca penned a line that remains one of the most accurate diagnoses of the human condition: "We suffer more often in imagination than in reality."
It is a profound truth that most of us experience daily. We don't just live our lives; we narrate them, preview them, and rewrite them in our minds. And more often than not, those mental drafts are horror stories.
The Anatomy of Imagined Suffering
Think about the last time you were dreading a difficult conversation, a performance review, or a medical appointment. You likely spent days beforehand playing out every worst-case scenario. Your heart raced, your stomach churned, and sleep evaded you.
In psychology, this is known as catastrophizing—an evolutionary survival mechanism gone rogue. Our brains evolved in an environment where anticipating danger (the rustle in the bushes might be a tiger) kept us alive.
But today, that same mechanism maps onto modern, abstract anxieties.
[Anticipated Event] ──> [Mental Projection of Worst-Case Scenario] ──> [Real Physiological Stress]
When you imagine a disaster, your brain doesn't always distinguish between the thought and the…
